quantitative.description: Arrangements and selections of objects in mathematical orders.

questions.question: In how many ways can the letters of the word 'LEADER' be arranged?
questions.explanation: The word 'LEADER' contains 6 letters, where the letter 'E' is repeated twice. Number of arrangements = 6! / 2! = (720) / 2 = 360 ways.

questions.question: Out of 7 consonants and 4 vowels, how many words of 3 consonants and 2 vowels can be formed?
questions.explanation: Number of ways of selecting 3 consonants from 7 = 7C3 = 35. Number of ways of selecting 2 vowels from 4 = 4C2 = 6. Total ways of selecting = 35 * 6 = 210. Each group of 5 letters can be arranged in 5! = 120 ways. Total words = 210 * 120 = 25,200.

quantitative.description: Cost price, selling price, profit percent, loss percent, and discounts.

questions.question: A person sells an article for $240 and incurs a loss of 20%. At what price should he sell it to gain 10%?
questions.explanation: Loss = 20%, Selling Price = $240. Therefore, Cost Price = 240 / 0.8 = $300. To gain 10%, new Selling Price = 300 * 1.1 = $330.

logical.description: Deductive reasoning questions based on statements and conclusions.

questions.question: Statements: (1) All mangoes are golden. (2) No golden things are cheap. Conclusions: (I) All mangoes are cheap. (II) No mangoes are cheap.
questions.explanation: Since all mangoes are golden, and no golden things are cheap, it is impossible for any mango to be cheap. Thus, conclusion II definitely follows.

logical.description: Decoding relationships and drawing family tree structures.

questions.question: Pointing to a photograph, a man said, 'I have no brother or sister but that man's father is my father's son.' Whose photograph was it?
questions.explanation: Since the speaker has no brother or sister, 'my father's son' must be the speaker himself. Thus, 'that man's father is the speaker'. The photograph is of his son.

verbal.description: Identifying grammatical errors and improving sentences.

questions.question: Identify the grammatically correct sentence: 'Neither of the two candidates were selected.'
options: Neither of the two candidates was selected.
Neither of the two candidates were selected.
Either of the two candidates were selected.
questions.answer: Neither of the two candidates was selected.
questions.explanation: 'Neither' acts as a singular pronoun, requiring the singular verb 'was' instead of the plural 'were'.
